speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two, survivor and War Crimes Trials participant is Lawrence Rhee. And the date April 9th, 1996. We left off, on the last tape, talking about leaving Recklinghausen. Is there anything else you'd like to say about before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I recall when-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the day Hitler came to power. Not came to power, was asked to take over. He came to power a little-- about two weeks later. We went ice skating with my classmates. And I broke my leg. And of course, I was taken to the hospital. And it so happened that my sister, my younger sister, had had an appendectomy that very same day. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us together in the same room. And when the windows were opened, a few days after, you know, we got to be on our own, we could hear the Hitler Youth march outside there, in the streets, and singing the song that, roughly translated, would go like this-- and when the Jews knife-- when the Jews' blood spurts off our knives, things go twice as well. That was the refrain. Can you sing it in German for us? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't-- I know the words.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm not good at singing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you say it in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, hearing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not pleasant. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not pleasant. Do you recall what you took with you when you left Recklinghausen? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take, personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were able to take all our furniture, all our belongings. Some of it you may-- right there is a piece in our house. We have the piano there. And we were able to take all our furniture, but only $10 apiece in money. That was for landing or being-- for registering in the United States at immigration time, at the time of immigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had your father already, at this point, lost his job? Or did he-- he own his own store?

speaker: interviewee
text: He owned his own store. But then things went from bad to worse. And he had to give up the store. And he became an insurance agent.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in-- you said Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: January, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall anything at all about the trip across the ocean? What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very pleasant trip. It was a small, small boat. And it was actually a combination of freighter and passengers. There were only about 20-or-so passengers. And it was a very pleasant trip. There was no prejudice that we discerned for-- by the part of the captain, or anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave other relatives in Germany when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you leave back in Germany, when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousins, uncles, aunts, the usual immediate family there. One of my aunts later on died in Theresienstadt. And I know I lost a third-- third cousin, I guess it would be, in a concentration camp. In fact, during the time of the Nuremberg Trials, I tried to locate him. I visited his mother. But I never was able to locate him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bernalillo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, you came to Los Angeles. And then you went directly to your uncle, was it, in Bernalillo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, straight-- in fact, one of my uncles met the ship. And we spent a day or two in Los Angeles, and then came by train to-- to Albuquerque-- in Albuquerque, New Mexico.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like for you? Can you recall back, leaving Germany and coming to America? What was your first recollections about being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --new in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: My English, of course, was horrible. There was a little of it. [LAUGHS] But my-- all my relatives, or most of them, my aunts and my cousins spoke some German. And we didn't have a difficult time to get started, initially. The first we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --were able, after settling in Bernalillo, my uncle got special permission to have my brother, myself, and my sister go to high school there for four weeks. That was stipulated, only four weeks. And to learn English and basic Spanish. Because when you lived in Bernalillo, in those days, you couldn't live without speaking Spanish. You got a mile or two away from the highway and nobody spoke English. In fact, I remember when going to school, the kids, our classmates, if they were caught speaking Spanish to each other, they were disciplined. Because English was at a very low percentage point yet. [LAUGHS] Often. And this was in a Catholic school, at a Sister school. The Sisters of Loretto, in-- ran the school in Bernalillo. So they were very nice to us. And we picked up what we picked up, and started from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss what was going on in Germany with any of your classmates? Or any of your relatives or friends when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all wanted to know. Everybody wanted to know what was going on. I mean, there's-- oh here's a German. Let's-- let's ask him. And everybody was interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do the first years that you arrived? Did you attend school or did you go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only four weeks in school. And then my uncles had a store in Bernalillo. And they put both of my brother and my-- all-- all three of us to work there. My other sister, my youngest sister, lived here in Albuquerque, with another uncle. And they were able to put her into a private school, where she learned English, of course, much faster than we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of store was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: General store. A trade-- actually, it was a trading post, the Bernalillo Mercantile Company, in Bernalillo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what you did. You went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to work, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for how many years, were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked with them for approximately two years. And then my uncle got me a job at the New Mexico Timber Company, which had a big sawmill in Bernalillo. And there I started to work for $0.24 an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enter the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- that's a little involved. Excuse me. In 1939, I think, they enacted the draft. And my brother volunteered. And eventually, he wound up in the 200th Coastal Artillery. It was an almost complete unit of New Mexico soldiers. And they were sent to Bataan. And he-- that was before the war, before Pearl Harbor. And then he lived through the March of Death. And he survived only-- only because he has a-- [? darned ?] for languages. And he picked up Japanese so quickly within about a year's time, that the Japanese needed him as an interpreter for the rest of the camp. Because they had needed the Japanese soldiers to do the fighting. So he was made the camp interpreter. And as a result, he was taken out of the rice paddies and was made a KP in the officers' mess, where he got a little bit more to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with you? Did you enter the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: I entered the-- at first I also tried to volunteer. But my eyesight was too poor. So in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --it was September or so, in 1941. I got a job offer to go to Oregon, to become a lumber grader in Oregon. And while there, Pearl Harbor occurred. And when we knew that Bataan of Corregidor had fallen. I wanted to go to the military so bad I could taste it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came back to New Mexico and volunteered. And I got into the Army, this time, by cheating a little. I memorized the first three or four lines of the eye test chart, and thus, passed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm guilty. [LAUGHS] So you enlisted? And that was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what branch of the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: I first was trained to be a medic. But after some time I heard in the camp, I was in camp-- stationed in the [INAUDIBLE] in Camp Barkeley, Texas, that they needed German-speaking personnel. And I tried to get in. And I succeeded getting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it the Army or the Navy, or the Marines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Army. Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the Army. And where did you receive your basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Camp Barkeley, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camp Barkeley, Texas. And that was for medical? Medic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was. Yeah, medic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you received any other-- then you said you went on to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Military intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Intelligence. Where were you trained for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp Ritchie, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a training was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it? Three, four, months. I forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you received that training, what rank were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a T5.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you were at Camp Ritchie, in Maryland, where did you then go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was sent-- from there, I was sent overseas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 1945, I believe it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent overseas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed in Glasgow. We went aboard a troopship, the New Amsterdam, which had been converted from the-- it was a luxury liner. And well, it had been converted to hold 9,000 troops. We were 17 of us in a state's cabin that was built for two. But we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first you were in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: England. And then from Glasgow, I went-- we were sent to Litchfield Barracks, which is near Birmingham, England. And from there, I was flown to Paris. Paris had already fallen. And from Paris-- we spent about a week or so in Paris, or less-- we was-- went by a truck to Baccarat, the famous glass place, with the fine glass. Of course, it was down at that time. And there I joined the 103rd Division, 409th regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: 103rd Division, 409th?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- the 409th regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the commanding officer? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was where, in Baccarat?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baccarat. And from there, we went fighting all across-- well, across France, Alsace-Lorraine, into Germany, and finally Innsbruck. And the day armistice was signed, we were in [? Schwarz, ?] Tirol or Austria, which was some 20, 30 miles east of Innsbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in actual combat yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, can you describe some of the battles you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this-- well, this was all across-- our duty is-- was to interrogate the prisoners of war to find out the enemy positions, so we could relay that to our division, so that they could make the proper plans for attacks or defense, or whatever it happened to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --so mine was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --interrogation of prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a weapon with you at all times?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a carbine. But we hardly ever used it. Because it-- ours was not so much of a front-line activity. Though we had some of it, of course. But it was to get the prisoners of war, interrogate them. And as I said, find out what the German positions were so we could relay that information to higher echelons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about the interrogation process? First of all, who were some of the prisoners that you interrogated? Was there anybody that stands out in your mind? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was one. But that was towards the end of the war, on the Siegfried line. But then let's start in the beginning. I always was of the opinion that if you treated a person right, you'll get more out of him. So I was a nonsmoker. And I was furnished, my GI pack, whatever it was-- I forget how often-- cigarettes. And I always started out by giving the prisoners a cigarette. And I had-- never from the rank and file. I got the information we needed without any difficulty. And I think the cigarette might have been a softener. [LAUGHS] Softener, soften-upper.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So they would freely give you the information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite freely. Only occasionally would they hesitate. But then we had techniques. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those techniques?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, convincing him that-- them-- that we knew, already, what he-- what he didn't want to say anyhow, let's say. We never hit a prisoner. We had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --well, maybe-- one case, I remember, we got a German sergeant who was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --very reluctant to give information. And it so happened that he had been wounded, or he said he had been wounded. I said, let me see what. So he pulled down his pants. And he had a-- a bullet has-- had grazed his legs and barely burned it. But not-- not opened, really-- opened up any flesh wounds. And it so happened that our first aid station was right below where we were interrogating, in-- in the same building. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I ask him, where is your-- they always carried their pay books on them. And I said-- he said, he didn't have any. And I thought that was odd.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him down. After the interrogation was over, took him down. And the medics took care of him. And one of the medics went through his pockets and found his pay book. And I said, how come you lied to me? He couldn't say a word. And then I opened the pay book. And in the pay book there was a list of all the passwords for the next week or so to get through the German lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of passwords were they? Do-- do you recall anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't. We got that information up to division so fast, we did-- that's-- because this was, of course, of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had hundreds and thousands of prisoners. No. That's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody famous or infamous that you-- that was a prisoner of war that you interrogated, that you can think of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know later on, at the Siegfried line, there was another odd incident. We-- little by-- this company of soldiers surrendered and came in by four and five and six, you know, as it-- as it goes. And they were all angry as could be because their lieutenant had not given them their rations of schnapps which was due the night before, but had kept them. And with a bunch of nurses, had rigged up a big party-- the German nurses, had rigged up a big party. And these guys were so mad. They told us everything. I mean, they were really-- [LAUGHS] and finally, they took the-- the-- the lieutenant himself was brought in. And he, in a very German way, said-- when we started interrogating him, he said, it is against the honor of a German officer to divulge military secrets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I shot back, is it also against the honor of a German officer to take the enlisted men's ration away and drink it up with a bunch of nurses?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] He literally collapsed. He gave us everything. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what regiment were you with, again? Can you just state it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 409th regiment. 109th Division. It was called the Cactus Division. We had an insignia of a cactus. Arizona, mostly Arizona and New Mexico people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall, at all, the-- any commanding officers, any names of any commanding officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my immediate commanding officer was Captain Woods. He was also interrogator. He was also a military intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your serial number? Do you-- can you recall?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you, coming back to Europe, and now speaking with people who were German, interrogating them? What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a sense of getting even. I had no feeling of being against my countrymen, you know, against the Germans. That was complete-- it was a sense of getting even for what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you advanced toward the Siegfried line. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went through. Our unit had to break through the Siegfried line. In fact, another incident, one of the pictures that I have will show you, where my friend and I were in a dugout. And our captain had been in that dugout too. But that particular day I had asked for permission to go up near the front lines, to get a better picture of what was going on so we could interrogate better. And while I was gone, our captain got hit, got wounded by-- in-- in that very dugout.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course I didn't know anything about it. And then the Regimental Commander came by and said, hey, what in the hell are you doing here? Well, I said, I had permission to come up here and see what you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you get your ass back over there where you belong. Captain Woods got hurt. Well, of course-- [LAUGHS] --I did it immediately. But I had not known about his being wounded. But pardon my English. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was your unit, or and were you, yourself, involved in liberating any concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our division was, but not I. I was not--

speaker: interviewer
text: The division was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The division as a whole, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The division consists of three regiments. And I was in one of the regiments. And then, you know, it's subdivided. It goes like a pyramid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what division were you again, just to clarify?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the 409th Division.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 409th. And your division was not involved directly with liberating? Am I understanding correctly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not I. I don't really know whether the-- the-- the front line, let's call it, the-- the-- the-- the lower echelons way up there were involved. But I know when we-- we were driving along with the jeep, and all of a sudden, we saw all those liberated people coming down the street. And we could hardly drive, there were so many of them. And we stopped the vehicle. And they kissed our clothes. And they kissed the jeep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said, what's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the-- but I, myself, was not involved in liberating a concentration camp. Were you friendly with the other interrogators?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We got along great. All-- we always worked together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone else from Germany like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my sergeant. In fact, that's a completely separate story. He had lived in Cologne, had studied medicine until he could no-- could go no further. He had a girlfriend. And he was able to get to the United States. I don't know what means he had. But his girlfriend was one of the passengers on the St. Louis. And eventually, there was-- you know, they tried to land in Cuba. And they tried to land in the United States. And President Roosevelt would not let them land. And so the ship went back. And she wound up in the Netherlands, in Holland. And when the war, the fighting had ended, my sergeant knew, somehow, how he knew her address. And he asked for permission to see her. [CRYING] And they were reunited in the Netherlands, and eventually got permission to marry. And they both live in Baltimore. And in fact, I went to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your sergeant's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Herman Mendels. And her name was, or is Gertrude. They live in Baltimore. And I went to see them when I went to the Nurembergers here a few-- two weeks ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything else-- any other incident that you can recall, or anything else you'd like to say about, while you were in military intelligence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was this one incident that-- that took place when-- at the time of Hitler's last birthday.
